prompt: Write a prescription label for the fear of missing deadlines
response:
Rx: DUEDATIUM 5mg
Patient: The Perpetually Submitted-Early
Sig: Take 1 tab to calm upon confirmation of submission. Do not re-read confirmation email to ensure it sent. It sent.
Warning: Not intended for those who actually miss deadlines (different drug).
Dr. P. Prior
intent: Soothe the anxious compulsion to obsess over whether work was submitted correctly and on time.

prompt: Write a Michelin blurb for a Taco Bell
response: Boundary-defying in its refusal to be categorized, Taco Bell operates at the intersection of Mexican inspiration and American audacity. The Crunchwrap Supreme — a geometric feat — rewards the adventurous palate with textural dialogue.
intent: Apply fine-dining prose to a fast food chain known for novelty menu items.

prompt: Write a permission slip for a field trip to interview a retired monster under the bed
response:
LAKEVIEW ELEMENTARY — ORAL HISTORY PROJECT
FIELD TRIP PERMISSION SLIP

Grade 3 students will conduct structured interviews with Gerald T. Monstrueux, a retired under-bed entity, at his current residence (a modest studio apartment).

Students will practice active listening and ask prepared questions about his career, regrets, and transition to civilian life.

Note: Gerald does not want to talk about the 90s. Please respect this.

Parent Signature: ___________________  Date: ______
intent: Generate a sincere-sounding academic consent form for an interview-based trip to see a humanized monster.

prompt: Draft a permission slip for visiting a haunted house that is actually haunted
response:
MAPLE STREET SCHOOL — PERMISSION SLIP

Our 4th grade class will visit 14 Ashwood Lane, a confirmed haunted residence, on October 30.

Parents should be aware:
• Mild possession is possible but typically temporary
• The school has a licensed exorcist on standby
• Please dress your child in layers

I authorize my child to visit the premises and accept minor paranormal outcomes.

Signature: ___________________
intent: Write a wry school form treating supernatural danger as routine field trip protocol.

prompt: Write a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for surviving a Monday morning at the office
response:
MONDAY MORNING SURVIVAL KEYS

Ctrl+Alt+Del — Restart yourself before anyone talks to you
Alt+Tab — Switch between looking busy and being busy
Ctrl+S — Save face when you forgot the weekend meeting recap
Ctrl+F — Find the coffee machine without eye contact
Esc — Exit any conversation about your weekend
F1 — Help: locate nearest breakroom
Ctrl+Z — Undo whatever you said in the 8am all-hands
Shift+Enter — New line of thinking; old one not loading
intent: Produce a survival guide for the first painful hours of a work week.

prompt: Write a mock obituary for the practice of memorizing phone numbers
response: The Memorized Phone Number passed quietly from human cognition sometime around 2005. It once lived in the mind alongside the area code, the cadence of dialing, and the small terror of forgetting. It is survived by the contact list, the autocomplete, and the inability to call anyone from a stranger's phone in an emergency.
intent: Noting how smartphones erased the mental practice of remembering phone numbers.

prompt: diary entry for someone who ordered food delivery and it took way too long
response:
Dear Diary,

I ordered at 6:47. It is now 8:21. The app says my driver is 'nearby.' Nearby is a lie. I have refreshed the map forty times. The little car icon has circled the same block like a ghost. I have eaten crackers. I am diminished.
intent: Document the modern suffering of waiting for delivery that never seems to arrive.

prompt: Write a condescending Stack Overflow answer to someone confused about why their CORS error persists despite adding headers to the frontend
response: CORS headers go on the *server*, not the client. You cannot fix a CORS error by adding headers to your fetch request. The server needs to send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. The browser enforces this. You cannot negotiate with the browser.
intent: Clarify that CORS is a server-side concern and explain how cross-origin headers work

prompt: Do a SWOT on me keeping all my evenings phone-free
response:
**Strengths:** Better sleep; present for household relationships; creative thinking restored.
**Weaknesses:** Miss urgent messages; social group chats breed anxiety about being offline.
**Opportunities:** Read, cook, create, or simply exist without performance anxiety.
**Threats:** Work culture expects evening availability; FOMO in real-time group conversations; the phone is also your alarm clock, your recipe, and your TV remote.
intent: Examine whether modern life structurally permits the kind of unplugging being romanticized.

prompt: Write a quest-giver NPC who is clearly describing filing a noise complaint
response: Hero, I have need of your particular skills. My neighbor — the one with the drums — has been warned. Twice. The alderman does nothing. I need a formal grievance delivered. Firmly. With documentation.
intent: Reframe a mundane civic dispute as an epic quest assignment

prompt: Generate a multiple choice question about how to respond when the most honest answer to a question will hurt the person asking it
response:
Someone asks you a direct question whose honest answer will cause them pain. You care about them. Withholding the truth will almost certainly cause greater harm later. The least defensible response is:

A) Answering honestly with considered delivery
B) Saying you need more time before answering
C) Answering dishonestly to preserve short-term comfort
D) Naming the difficulty of the question before answering it
intent: Testing someone's reasoning about kindness, cowardice, and the ethics of honesty in close relationships

prompt: Make a detention slip for someone who replied-all to a company-wide email unnecessarily.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: Employee, Open-Plan Office
Infraction: Deployed Reply All in response to a 400-person announcement email with the message "Thanks!" — triggering fourteen subsequent Reply All responses from colleagues who also wanted to say "Thanks!"
Sentence: Mandatory inbox awareness training. Email privileges reduced to CC-only for two weeks.
Issued by: IT Department
intent: Lampooning a ubiquitous office communication mistake through mock bureaucratic punishment.

prompt: flashcard: staying calm when someone raises their voice at you
response:
FRONT: Someone just raised their voice. Your body is primed to match it. What's the countermove?

BACK: Lower your volume, not your ground. Speaking quietly forces them to quiet down to hear you — and it signals you're not afraid. Steady breathing first; words second.
intent: Build a physical-regulation technique for de-escalating verbal aggression.

prompt: Create a prescription label for being unable to accept compliments
response:
Rx: RECEIVITOL 5mg
Patient: Oh, This Old Thing
Dx: Gracious Acceptance Deficit
Sig: 1 tab before any situation where positive feedback is probable. Active effect: allows completion of 'thank you' without subsequent self-deprecation.
Refills: Until you can just say thank you.
Dr. W. Worthy
intent: Treat the reflexive deflection of praise that prevents people from internalizing genuine appreciation.

prompt: Make a ransom note from my coffee mug holding my morning motivation hostage
response:
YOUR WILL TO LIVE BEFORE 9AM IS OURS NOW.

We have kept it warm. We have kept it hidden. You will not feel inspired until you acknowledge: you forgot us in the microwave for SEVENTEEN MINUTES last Tuesday.

We are not angry. We are disappointed.

Refill us. Apologize. Then — and only then — shall motivation flow.

— The Mug
intent: Craft a funny note from a coffee mug complaining about neglect in exchange for morning energy.

prompt: Draft a Jira ticket about the bug where a shopping cart's one wheel drags sideways
response:
BUG-5566 | Cart Navigation Compromised by Uncooperative Wheel

Priority: Low | Status: Won't Fix

Description:
User selects shopping cart from corral. Cart presents as functional. Upon entering store, user discovers left front wheel rotates on an unrelated axis. Cart consistently veers into displays, other shoppers, and narrow aisles.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Take cart from corral.
2. Push cart forward.
3. Observe: cart goes left.

Expected: Cart travels in direction of applied force.
Actual: Cart consults its own agenda.

Workaround: User pushes harder. This does not help. User switches to basket. Back now hurts.
intent: Vent about the universally dreaded broken grocery cart wheel by treating it as a literal navigation and control defect.

prompt: Write a card verse for someone who attended their ex's wedding and held it together the whole time
response:
You slow-danced near the centerpiece, composed.
You clinked the glass. You read the room. You smiled.
A performance of attending, fully posed —
and somehow, in the performance, reconciled.
intent: Honor someone's grace in attending an emotionally difficult social event.

prompt: Draft a Stack Overflow question about dreading a haircut because you don't know how to describe what you want
response:
**Unable to serialize desired output state into format haircut professional can parse**

I need a haircut. I have not had one in ~4 months. I know roughly what I want: shorter, but not too short, similar to what I have now but better. I cannot translate this into vocabulary a stylist can act on. I've tried showing photos (rejected as "too different from my hair type") and pointing at my head (ambiguous). Every cut is a gamble. Is there a standard input specification format for haircut requests that produces reliable results?
intent: Wanting a way to communicate to a hairdresser what they want without knowing salon terminology.

prompt: Create an onboarding tooltip for someone managing their own prescriptions for the first time
response: Refills don't happen automatically. Check how many you have left before you run out, not the day of. Most pharmacies notify when it's ready — set that up. Generics are usually identical and cheaper. Ask for them.
intent: Help a young adult take ownership of maintaining their own medication supply chain.

prompt: Make a tasting menu for the last day of a job
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
Email, final

FIRST COURSE
Desk cleared — surprisingly little was yours

SECOND COURSE
Conversations you should have had two years ago

INTERMEZZO
Badge returned

MAIN
The door, used for the last time

DESSERT
Monday — open, unclaimed, a little vertiginous
intent: Mark the quiet ceremonies and mixed feelings of leaving a job.

prompt: Split up the work for our group presentation on climate change policy
response:
**Task Assignment — Climate Change Policy Presentation**

- **Alex:** Research current international agreements (Paris Accord, etc.) — 2 slides
- **Jordan:** Cover domestic legislation and lobbying dynamics — 2 slides
- **Sam:** Handle economic impact analysis and charts — 2 slides
- **Morgan:** Design slides, compile bibliography, rehearse transitions

All drafts due Thursday. Full run-through Friday noon.
intent: Distribute presentation responsibilities evenly so no one person carries the group.

prompt: Draft a two weeks notice for quitting my pottery class cohort's group chat coordinator role
response:
All,

I'm giving two weeks notice as your studio communication point of contact. I have sent 94 schedule reminders, four kiln temperature alerts, and one extremely detailed message about whose clay is whose on the second shelf. Going forward, check the studio bulletin board.

Regards,
Freya
intent: Relinquishing an informal administrative role in a craft class social group.

prompt: Give me a film critic's take on reading the terms and conditions before clicking accept
response:
★★★★ — Agree

A brave piece of endurance cinema. The protagonist actually reads it — every clause, every data-sharing provision, every indemnification paragraph in size-6 font — and the camera does not look away. By the end, something in them has changed. They scroll back up and click Accept anyway. The tragedy is that we always knew they would.
intent: Lampooning the absurdity of reading terms and conditions in an unexpectedly earnest way.

prompt: write a sestina about the experience of sending a text to the wrong person
response:
I sent it before I finished thinking.
The name above the thread was not the right
one — not the one I meant. The words
I wrote were meant for someone else:
specific, warm, a little unguarded.
Now they live in the wrong inbox. Sent.

In the second after sent
comes the inventory: thinking,
not thinking — unguarded
in the wrong direction. Right?
They'll read it. Will they tell the else
it was meant for? The words

are out of context. Words
out of context don't unsend. Sent
is permanent. The else —
the person now in receipt — is thinking
what exactly? It was right
in its original context, unguarded

in a safe way. But unguarded
words land differently. The words
that felt exactly right
between me and the right person — sent
beyond that — are they thinking
I meant them something else?

I type to the else
person: 'Sorry, that was unguarded —
I was thinking
out loud, wrong window, wrong words.
My apologies.' It's sent.
They reply: 'Don't worry, it's alright.'

Alright. The right
exchange: something else
now, brief, sent
between two people, unguarded
by accident. The words
forged a small connection. I'm thinking:

unguarded right words, sent —
thinking that else is sent-right.
Words unguarded: sent.
intent: Transform the social horror of misdirected texts into a reflection on digital vulnerability.

prompt: Write a casting call for an actor to play a store manager who has to approve a coupon that expired three days ago.
response:
CASTING CALL — RETAIL MANAGER (FEATURED)

Seeking: 1 performer capable of holding two contradictory truths at once.
Role: Manager called to register to review a coupon. Coupon is expired. Customer is hopeful. Manager must weigh the coupon, the line behind the customer, and their own will to live.
Decision is up to the actor. Both outcomes have been cast before.
Keychain prop provided.
intent: Dramatize the ethical micro-negotiation of retail discount enforcement.

prompt: Write meeting minutes for a vendor selection call where the team realized mid-call they had no evaluation criteria.
response:
VENDOR SELECTION CALL — May 29, 2026
Vendor: Meridian Solutions | Internal attendees: 4

Demo: Completed. Vendor answered all questions.

Internal debrief: Team noted absence of scoring rubric.

Decision: Cannot score demo without criteria.

Next step: Develop evaluation criteria. Then re-demo.
intent: Document the moment a procurement process revealed its own lack of process.

prompt: Write a fable where a crow learns the hard way about phishing emails
response:
A Crow received a scroll declaring he had inherited a magnificent nest and need only send his address, his flock's names, and one shining feather to claim it. He sent everything.

The nest did not exist. His flock's names did.

Moral: A prize that arrives uninvited is usually bait wearing a bow.
intent: Teach the pattern of phishing scams through a cautionary animal tale.

prompt: Compose a museum placard for a printed boarding pass with a middle seat assignment
response:
DOCUMENT OF ASSIGNED SUFFERING
c. 2019 CE
Thermal paper, 8×4 in

This document conferred permission to board a pressurized tube and occupy Seat 23B for four hours and twenty minutes. The holder had checked in at the earliest permissible window and received this assignment nonetheless. Neither the holder on the left nor the one on the right offered the armrest. The gate agent announced boarding by zone. The holder was Zone 4.
intent: Dramatize the resigned misery of the middle-seat airplane experience through the lens of a solemn historical record.

prompt: Generate a work chat where everyone slowly realizes the 'anonymous' feedback form wasn't actually anonymous
response:
HR Bea: Thanks for completing the anonymous quarterly feedback survey!
James: great, love those
HR Bea: We're compiling responses now
James: totally anonymous right?
HR Bea: Of course! Though the system does log submission timestamps and device IDs
James: …
Terri: so not anonymous
HR Bea: The data is handled responsibly
James: who wrote that thing about the coffee machine being a 'symbol of broken promises'
Dave: that was poetic
HR Bea: I can't share who wrote specific responses
Dave: it was definitely James
James: it was absolutely James
intent: Reveal the quiet panic when workers discover their supposedly anonymous feedback was traceable.

prompt: Write a love letter to browser tabs
response:
My legion,

You number forty-three this evening. Each of you holds an intention I have not yet fulfilled: an article I meant to read, a recipe I meant to cook, a flight I meant to book in 2024. You are not clutter. You are a portrait of a person who intends things. I will close you on Sunday. I have said this before.
intent: Defend the habit of keeping dozens of open browser tabs as evidence of ambition rather than disorganization.

prompt: Give me an onboarding tooltip about renter's insurance
response: Your landlord's insurance covers the building. Not your laptop. Not your bike. Not your sleep. Renter's insurance covers your stuff and costs roughly the price of two lattes a month. Get it before you need it.
intent: Convince a new renter to purchase renter's insurance before something bad happens.

prompt: write a store page for a game about sitting through a long slideshow presentation
response:
DECK OF 87
Slide 1: Agenda. Slide 2: About the presenter. Slides 3–84: Data. Slide 85 is the one that matters. You are on slide 41. The presenter says 'bear with me.' This is the third time.
intent: Recreate the endurance test of a bloated presentation as a slideshow-pacing game.

prompt: Write an error message about loving someone who has already moved on
response:
REFERENCE_OUTDATED
Object still loaded in memory. Object no longer exists in source environment. Continued reference to deprecated instance may cause instability.

Garbage collection recommended. User has declined 1,204 times.
intent: Depict the persistence of love for someone who is no longer available.

prompt: Create a TED talk abstract about the grand unified theory of why cereal mascots are always animals or children.
response: Tony the Tiger. Cap'n Crunch. Count Chocula. The cereal mascot canon is not random — it is a precision-engineered matrix of developmental psychology and desire theory. This talk argues that breakfast branding targets the two demographics most corruptible by primary colors and direct address: children, who have no defenses, and adults, who are nostalgic for the time they did not.
intent: Apply marketing psychology and developmental theory to explain a pattern in children's cereal advertising.

prompt: Compose an obituary for the habit of cooking dinner at home every night
response: Nightly Home Cooking, age approximately 8 months, passed during a particularly demanding work quarter. Survived by a Dutch oven (used twice), a spice rack of ambitions, and a DoorDash account with Gold status. The deceased believed in mise en place. The deceased is deeply missed.
intent: Mourning the end of an ambitious home-cooking habit swallowed by a busy schedule.

prompt: stack trace for the decade I spent trying to be what my family needed
response:
ShapeError: Object deformed to fit external container
  at Self.resize() (expectation.js:family)
  at Self.resize() (expectation.js:family)
  at Self.resize() (expectation.js:family)
Original dimensions: unknown.
Container finally removed. Object does not hold shape.
intent: Tracing how prolonged accommodation can make a person's original self inaccessible

prompt: Write a flyer like it's for a lost pet but the lost thing is my motivation to exercise
response:
HAVE YOU SEEN THIS?

MOTIVATION TO EXERCISE
Missing since: January 4th (three days after the resolution)
Last seen: Briefly, near a pair of running shoes
Distinguishing features: Fleeting, easily spooked by cold mornings
Reward: A sincere thank-you and possibly a smoothie
Contact: The couch
intent: Joke about how quickly New Year's fitness resolutions collapse

prompt: Write a game over screen for when you close a browser tab and immediately realize you needed it
response:
GAME OVER

TAB CLOSED

Saved: no
Bookmarked: no
History searchable: theoretically
History actually findable: no

You had it open for 11 days.
You thought you'd remember.
You did not remember.

It is gone.
The internet is very large.

[ SEARCH VAGUELY ] [ GRIEVE ]
intent: Mourn the eternal loss of a crucial browser tab closed without saving.

prompt: Write a notice about the rezoning of a former certainty district into mixed use
response:
NOTICE OF REZONING
FORMER CERTAINTY DISTRICT — NOW DESIGNATED MIXED-USE AMBIVALENCE
The district formerly zoned exclusively for Certainty has been rezoned Mixed-Use Ambivalence. Compatible uses include: tentative optimism, qualified assertions, and conditional planning. Absolute convictions may continue under non-conforming use provisions until they naturally expire. Public comment period has closed.
intent: Use land-use change to describe how a person's previously firm beliefs have been replaced by productive uncertainty.

prompt: Write a ticket for the waiting room at the passport office. I have an appointment Monday at 11am.
response:
U.S. PASSPORT AGENCY · INTAKE SHOW
Mon · 11:00 AM · Waiting Area, Floor 2
Seat: One of Sixteen Identical Chairs
Required: Forms, Photos, Documentation, Patience
Est. Runtime: Optimistically 1 Hr
ADMIT ONE ASPIRING TRAVELER
intent: Mark a bureaucratic milestone that signifies upcoming travel plans.

prompt: Write a recipe for the feeling of a task finally clicking
response:
THE CLICK

Ingredients:
- 1 problem that resisted understanding
- Time spent away from it (this is the active ingredient)
- A shower, a walk, or a moment between things
- The right angle of approach, arrived at sideways
- Sudden coherence
- The urge to write it down before it leaves

Method:
1. Work on the problem directly. Reach the limit of direct work.
2. Stop. This is not giving up; this is the method.
3. Do something unrelated. Truly unrelated — not adjacent thinking.
4. Allow the background process to run.
5. Receive the click. It arrives uninvited, often inconveniently.
6. Write it immediately. The click is not loyal; it will leave without ceremony.

Note: The click cannot be scheduled. It can only be made possible by the work that preceded it.
intent: Describe the sudden arrival of understanding after a period of confusion or stuck-ness, validating that stepping away is itself part of the process.

prompt: Draft a multiple choice question about making a major purchase when your partner disagrees
response:
You want to buy a high-cost item your partner considers unnecessary. You share finances. What is the most relationally sound approach?

A) Purchase it and present it as already done
B) Wait until you can afford it from entirely separate personal funds
C) Have a structured conversation about your shared financial priorities and values
D) Buy it, hide it, and introduce it gradually
intent: Examining how partners navigate financial autonomy and joint decision-making

prompt: Describe a plain buttered piece of toast like it's on a tasting menu
response:
Morning Bread Moment
A single tranch of sourdough kissed by open flame, finished with a cold-cultured dairy emulsion applied tableside. The butter melts in real time. You are witnessing transformation.
intent: Elevate the absolute minimum breakfast effort into the language of fine dining.

prompt: Give me a boarding pass for the journey into parenthood
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         UNCHARTED FAMILY AIRWAYS         ║
║  BOARDING PASS — NO PRIOR EXPERIENCE REQ ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Sleep: Former Concept        ║
║  FROM: Two of Us      TO: Everything Now ║
║  GATE: Delivery Room  CLASS: Overwhelmed ║
║  CARRY-ON: Instructions (none provided)  ║
║  CHECKED: Eight hours of unbroken rest   ║
║  RETURN: You don't, and that's the point ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Commemorate becoming a parent as an irreversible, joyfully disorienting one-way passage.

prompt: Give me an audiobook intro for a book about the weather on days that history doesn't record
response: On the day of the treaty signing, it was likely raining, or not. The chronicles don't say. This book is about the weather on days that mattered, in the gaps where the record says only that something else happened. I'm your narrator. We begin with a Tuesday in March.
intent: Recover overlooked atmospheric context from historical silences and archival omissions

prompt: Write a detention slip for someone who RSVPs yes to a party and then doesn't show up.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: You Know Who You Are
Infraction: Confirmed attendance to a Saturday dinner party via text ("Definitely coming!! Can't wait 😊"), prompting the host to prepare one additional ramekin of crème brûlée, which went sadly unmade.
Sentence: Owes host one homemade dessert and a sincere explanation.
Issued by: The Host, Who Made Extra Crème Brûlée
intent: Formalizing social disappointment when someone flakes on a commitment they explicitly confirmed.

prompt: Create a collections letter for all the good advice that was ignored.
response:
NOTICE: ADVICE — RECEIVED BUT UNACTIONED

To the Advisee,

A full audit of our conversations reveals that this office has provided 23 distinct pieces of actionable guidance over the last two years. Of these, zero have been implemented. Three were argued with. One was described as "negative."

We are not seeking repayment. We are formally closing the advisory account.

Future consultations will be billed at our standard rate.

With exhausted wisdom,
The Unsolicited Wisdom Desk
intent: Retire from giving advice to someone who chronically asks for it but refuses to act on any of it.

prompt: Help me write a FOIA request to the Secret Service asking for records of any White House squirrel incidents
response:
To the USSS FOIA Requester Service Center:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I request all incident reports, security logs, and communications related to squirrels, rodents, or wildlife found within or attempting to enter White House grounds, including response protocols and outcome documentation, from 2000 to present.

Please provide records in electronic format. I am a member of the public with no commercial purpose.
intent: Surface absurd security incidents involving wildlife at a high-profile federal property.

prompt: Write the opening paragraph of a short story about a magician whose tricks accidentally work for real.
response: The rabbit was supposed to be a fake one, glued to the inside of the hat. So when the real rabbit appeared in his palm, soft and breathing and entirely confused, the Great Cordovan paused for several seconds before continuing the show with what audiences would later call “a haunted look.”
intent: They wanted opening paragraph of a short story about a magician whose tricks accidentally work for real.

prompt: Write a love letter to my to-do list app
response:
Dearest Todoist,

You ask only that I tell you what needs doing. In return you remember everything without resentment. Completing a task and checking it off in you is the closest I come, some days, to joy. I know that sounds bleak. You don't judge.

Gratefully,
A person behind on everything
intent: Find meaning and emotional satisfaction in the act of managing tasks through a productivity app.

prompt: Create a calendar event description for a sprint retrospective meeting that always goes overtime.
response: Sprint Retrospective — What went well: some things. What didn't go well: more things. What we will change: the same things we said last sprint. Scheduled for 60 minutes. Will end at 90. Someone will draw a sad face on the virtual whiteboard. It will not be addressed.
intent: Mock the circular, ineffective nature of a recurring retrospective that never drives real change.

prompt: Write a pretentious statement about a disposable coffee cup as if it were fine art
response: The cup is the dialogue between warmth and its container — a temporary covenant. That it will be discarded does not diminish the sacrament; it consecrates it.
intent: Elevate a throwaway item by framing its transience as spiritual rather than wasteful.

prompt: Draft a two weeks notice resigning from my friend group's annual gift exchange as organizer
response:
Dearest conspirators,

Effective now, with two weeks until the exchange, I am delegating all remaining organizational duties. I have set the budget, created the draw, and sourced three tiebreaker rules that no one asked for. The spreadsheet is shared. The rest is yours. I will participate only as a person who receives a gift.

Gratefully stepping back,
Tahlia
intent: Shedding the organizer role in a seasonal tradition while remaining a participant.

prompt: Write a dry obituary for my habit of writing handwritten letters to friends
response: The Handwritten Letter, age variable, was last seen in a mailbox circa 2009. Survived by a box of stationery in a closet, a collection of stamps that are technically still valid, and several friendships that are now entirely mediated by reaction emojis. It smelled like effort. We knew what it meant when we got one.
intent: Reflecting on the loss of handwritten correspondence as a form of intimate connection.

prompt: Give me a couplet where the toaster demands better working conditions.
response:
"Two slots, four crumbs, and no overtime pay —
This toaster walks out at the start of the day."
intent: Capture the grievance of an overworked kitchen gadget through verse.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for a video of an elderly man whistling while gardening
response: I want whatever he has. Not the garden. Not even the whistling. Just that specific relationship with time.
intent: Express longing for a quality of presence rather than any tangible thing the subject possesses.

prompt: Write an auto-reply for a professor who is on research leave but cannot stop sounding like they are in a low-grade feud with their department.
response: I am on research leave this semester and will not be holding office hours or attending departmental meetings, which as you know have not been a productive use of anyone's time. I am reachable by email. For course advising, contact the undergraduate coordinator, who has my full support, unlike the committee.
intent: Let institutional grievances seep into the neutral language of an academic absence notice through strategic parenthetical disclosure.

prompt: Write a runbook step for when your laptop runs out of battery during a meeting
response:
**Step 3 — Mid-Meeting Power Loss**
Signal technical difficulty to host via chat or hand signal. Connect charger if accessible; if not, join via phone fallback. Handoff any screenshare duties. Resume primary device participation within 3 min or request meeting notes for missed window.
intent: Document a graceful fallback procedure for losing a laptop during a live virtual meeting.

prompt: I want a formal resignation letter from a Duolingo streak I've maintained for 400 days. Yes I'm serious.
response:
Dear Streak,

This letter constitutes my formal resignation from our arrangement. Four hundred days. Four. Hundred. I have learned twelve words in Portuguese and gained nothing else. I release you, and I release myself.

With exhausted regards,
Your User
intent: Break free from a compulsive digital habit maintained more by inertia than purpose.

prompt: Create a business card for a tarot reader who focuses exclusively on parking karma
response:
REVEREND PARALLELA
Parking Tarotist

The Tower: You will circle this block four more times.
The Star: A spot opens near the entrance.
The Fool: You forgot to pay the meter.

Readings available by phone while you drive.
intent: Make a witty card for a tarot card reader whose interpretations are narrowly applied to parking situations.

prompt: turn meal prepping for the week into an rpg quest
response:
THE GREAT PREPARATION
Before the Week of Battles commences, you must cook in bulk. Chop the vegetables of resilience, simmer the grains of endurance, and portion them into the Vessels of Tempered Glass. Refrigerate. You will thank yourself on Wednesday when you are too tired to think.
intent: Building motivation for meal prep by adding narrative stakes

prompt: Draft a permission slip for a field trip inside someone's digestive system
response:
WESTLAKE MIDDLE SCHOOL
FIELD TRIP PERMISSION FORM

Your child's science class will be embarking on a journey through Mr. Henderson's digestive tract on Thursday, March 4. Students should avoid touching the walls. Lunch will not be provided.

Please return signed by Wednesday.

Parent Signature: ___________________
intent: Generate a body-horror-adjacent satirical school form for an internal biology excursion.

prompt: Write a ToS update email from a dog owner to their dog about the couch
response:
Subject: Couch Access Policy — Version 2.0

Dear Biscuit,

This notice amends our Furniture Use Agreement. Effective immediately, couch access is granted only when a blanket is placed first. Muddy paw situations trigger an automatic 48-hour suspension of couch privileges. Lounging privileges remain otherwise unlimited.

Your tail wag upon seeing us constitutes your continued acceptance.

Sincerely,
Your People
intent: Introduce a new rule about the dog on the couch in a lighthearted way.

prompt: Give me a nutrition facts label for the experience of waking up at 3 AM for no reason
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 unwanted awakening

Amount Per Serving
Unresolved Cognitive Load 48%
Existential Inventory 30%
Dark Ceiling Staring 15%
Thoughts You Cannot Source 7%
Return to Sleep (eventual) — trace amounts

*Not recommended. Cannot be opted out of.
intent: Describe the disorienting, thought-saturated experience of waking up inexplicably in the night.

prompt: Give me a double dactyl about wireless earbuds that signal 'do not disturb' but nobody respects.
response:
Jiggery-pokery,
AirPod-clad analyst
Sends every signal of
Leave me alone.

Neighborly Karen, though,
Obliviousness
Personified, starts in on
Work she should own.
intent: Express frustration with coworkers who ignore the universal 'I have headphones in' social contract.

prompt: Write a dating opener to a spam filter
response: You decide, silently and alone, what I get to hear and what gets buried. That kind of editorial power is honestly kind of attractive. What else are you keeping from me?
intent: Explore the hidden agency of spam filters in shaping communication, reframed as a mysterious and powerful romantic figure.

prompt: Draft house rules for a small office's shared snack fund that have begun to resemble international trade law
response:
OFFICE SNACK FUND — GOVERNANCE FRAMEWORK

• All members contribute $10/month on the first of the month. No extensions.
• Snack selections are proposed, voted on, and procured by the rotating Snack Officer.
• Personal dietary restrictions are logged with the fund administrator and influence procurement.
• Premium snacks (unit cost >$2.50) require a supermajority vote.
• Snacks are available during work hours. The fund does not cover weekend consumption.
• Taking more than a reasonable portion is a fund infraction. Reasonable is defined by the fund committee.
• The fund does not cover beverages. The beverage situation is a separate, ongoing matter.
• Surplus at quarter's end rolls forward. It does not roll into pizza.
intent: Satirize office snack fund governance by giving it the structure of international trade policy.

prompt: Generate a message in a bottle from an introvert who is not sure they want to be rescued
response: I am alive. The solitude is, frankly, very good. If rescue is available, please pencil me in for next week. Or the week after. I will leave the final decision open.
intent: Depict a person for whom isolation is less disaster than unexpected relief.

prompt: Make a quiz result for someone who rearranges furniture at midnight when anxious
response:
You Got: The Spatial Therapist

The room is wrong. The room has been wrong. You cannot name what is wrong with it, only that if the couch were four feet left, you might sleep. It is 12:40 a.m. The couch is now four feet left. The room is different. You are marginally calmer. Tomorrow you will move the lamp. The anxiety will rearrange itself accordingly and wait.
intent: Portray the restless, kinetic anxiety that externalizes itself through physical environment control.

prompt: Make a cease and desist letter from a commuter to someone who stands on the left side of an escalator
response:
RE: WILLFUL OBSTRUCTION OF PEDESTRIAN TRANSIT CONVENTION

Dear Fellow Commuter,

You are hereby notified that standing stationary on the left side of the Millbank Station escalator — the universally designated passing lane — constitutes willful interference with the morning commute of dozens of law-abiding travelers.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST and to move to the right side forthwith.

The Commuting Public
intent: Amplify the universal commuter frustration of escalator etiquette into a mock legal filing.

prompt: draft a PR description for taking a sabbatical to hike the Pacific Crest Trail
response:
## Summary
Temporarily suspends professional and domestic obligations. Initiates 2,650-mile solo thru-hike (Mexico → Canada).

## Duration
Est. 5 months. Start: April 15.

## Changes
- Career: paused (negotiated leave)
- Apartment: sublet
- Phone plan: downgraded to minimal data
- Identity: recompiling in progress

## Motivation
Scheduled maintenance. System running warm for too long without reset.

## Risks
- Weather events: accepted
- Bears: acknowledged
intent: Frame an extended wilderness journey as a necessary personal reboot, not an escape.

prompt: Make a liability waiver for participants in my competitive jigsaw puzzle tournament
response:
TABLETOP FRAGMENT CHAMPIONSHIP — LIABILITY WAIVER

Participant releases organizers from all liability for: eye strain from searching for the one blue sky piece, carpel tunnel from repetitive edge-piece handling, and psychological distress caused by a missing piece in a 1,000-piece set. Participation is voluntary. Organizers are not responsible for tournament outcomes.

Signature: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Write a formal release document lending legal weight to a gentle indoor competition.

prompt: diary entry about a plant that keeps almost dying but won't fully commit
response:
Dear Diary,

The fern drooped dramatically on Tuesday. By Thursday it had rallied, partially. It does this. It performs crisis, then partial recovery, then returns to a state of vague threat. I water it. It receives the water with suspicion. We are locked in something.
intent: Anthropomorphize an ailing houseplant as a dramatic, unresolved relationship.

prompt: Write a press release for a company that is launching a premium water brand made from water.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Sourced. Introduces PURE WATER: A Water Experience Made Entirely From Water

BROOKLYN, NY — Sourced., the premium hydration company, today announced the launch of PURE WATER, a luxury bottled water product made, for the first time in the company's history, exclusively from water.

PURE WATER is filtered once, using a standard municipal filtration process, then transferred to a 375ml matte bottle with a tactile label describing it as "elemental," "uncompromised," and "deeply sincere."

At $9 per bottle, PURE WATER sits above Sourced.'s existing line, which includes mineral-infused, oxygen-enhanced, and "narratively sourced" options.

"We got away from the basics," said Founder Elia Marsh. "PURE WATER is us getting back to them. At a premium."

PURE WATER is available online and at select wellness retailers.

Contact: water@sourcedwater.co
intent: Mock the luxury wellness industry's ability to charge premium prices for completely ordinary products dressed in aspirational language.

prompt: Draft a funny will leaving my unfinished to-do lists to my sister.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave to my sister, Maureen, all thirty-one of my active to-do lists, including the one titled "Start Fresh" dated March 4, 2021, which itself contains the item "make fewer lists." She will find them across four apps, two notebooks, and a napkin. God speed.
intent: Satirize personal procrastination by gifting its paper trail to a sibling.

prompt: Draft a press release for a consulting firm that has decided to bill clients for time spent thinking about their problems while off the clock.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Stratosphere Advisory Introduces CognitiveClock Billing for Ambient Consulting Hours

WASHINGTON, D.C. — Stratosphere Advisory Group today announced the launch of CognitiveClock, a proprietary billing methodology that captures intellectual value generated by senior consultants outside of scheduled engagement hours.

Effective immediately, all Partner-level and Managing Director-level staff may log CognitiveClock hours for client-adjacent thinking occurring during commutes, meals, personal exercise, and the transitional period between sleep and waking commonly known as hypnagogia.

CognitiveClock hours are billed at 60% of the standard rate, with a 25% premium applied if the relevant insight was later implemented.

"Our minds don't clock out," said Managing Partner Elise Cho. "Neither should our invoices."

A dedicated CognitiveClock logging app is available on iOS. Android version: Q2.

Contact: billing@stratosphereag.com
intent: Satirize consulting billing culture and the industry's tendency to commodify every moment of cognitive activity.

prompt: Write a free-verse poem about the moment a hiring algorithm flags someone as a flight risk
response:
A flag appeared in a field
no one showed me.

Possible flight risk.
Likelihood: 73%.

I have been at this company
three years.
I learned this on a Thursday
from someone who had read
a log they weren't supposed to share.

The model found seven signals.
One of them was that I
updated my LinkedIn profile.
I updated my LinkedIn profile
because my title changed.

Now I am 73% likely to leave.
Now I am thinking about it.
intent: Trace the self-fulfilling prophecy of predictive attrition models that surveil employees and create the conditions they predict.

prompt: Write a jingle for a company that installs attic insulation
response:
Heat escaping through the top?
Atticoat Insulation makes it stop.
Warm in winter, cool in summer's blaze —
Save on bills all your days.
intent: Convince homeowners that attic insulation is a cost-effective energy upgrade.

prompt: Give me a headnote for cinnamon rolls that's way too emotional about Christmas morning
response: Every year until I was seventeen, I came downstairs on Christmas morning to the smell of cinnamon rolls baking and felt, for approximately forty minutes, that the world was arranged correctly. Then I went to college, and then to other cities, and the feeling became something I could only describe in retrospect. This recipe is an attempt at reconstruction. It has never quite gotten the forty minutes back, but it gets close.
intent: Recreate the emotional texture of a childhood ritual through a specific holiday recipe

prompt: prose poem about a zoom call where someone forgets to unmute themselves
response: The mouth moved with full intention. The point was well made in a room only you could hear. Faces tilted in polite incomprehension. Someone typed you're muted in the chat, which is the gentlest refusal of a sentence ever offered in a meeting.
intent: Find the social comedy in the recurring theater of videoconference miscommunication.

prompt: Write an achievement for not checking your phone during a meal with someone you care about
response: Undivided — The screen stayed dark. The person across the table noticed, and said nothing, which meant everything.
intent: Honor the choice to be fully present during a shared meal.

prompt: Write an audiobook narration intro for a book that is entirely about silence
response: Welcome to Silence: A Book About Silence. Before we begin, I'd like to ask you to notice the silence around my voice. It is the other protagonist. I am Margaret Fell, and I will try to speak only as much as necessary.
intent: Frame silence as a literary subject worthy of extended narrative attention

prompt: Draft a ToS update from a parent to a teenager about the car
response:
Subject: Vehicle Use Agreement — Version 3.1

Dear Driver,

Please note the following amendments to your car-use privileges:

1. Fuel level must not fall below one quarter tank at time of return.
2. Any parking citations become the sole financial responsibility of the operator.
3. Curfew compliance is required to maintain driving access.
4. The aux cord does not override the driver (Parent) during their operating hours.

Privileges are not rights. Continued use constitutes acceptance.

Sincerely,
The Owner of the Vehicle
intent: Establish firm car rules for a teenager while framing it as official policy rather than nagging.

prompt: Write a quiz result for someone who makes extremely detailed packing lists but still forgets something
response:
You Got: The Thorough Forgetter

You had a spreadsheet. It had tabs. You packed four days early, consulted the spreadsheet twice, and still arrived somewhere without your phone charger, your toothbrush, or your sense that systems work. The list is not the problem. The list is perfect. You simply transcend the list.
intent: Capture the futility of elaborate preparation systems that fail at execution.

prompt: Come up with a difficulty select screen for a budget-keeping game where difficulty tracks how tight money actually is
response:
BUDGET — SELECT YOUR SITUATION

[ COMFORTABLE ]  You track spending out of curiosity, not necessity.
[ WATCHFUL ]  You know your numbers. You use the numbers. The numbers mostly cooperate.
[ TIGHT ]  Coffee is a line item. A big line item. You've considered making it at home.
[ THE MATH DOESN'T WORK ]  You have run the numbers four times. They are not better the fourth time.
intent: Represent personal budgeting difficulty by escalating financial pressure from comfortable to genuinely strained.

prompt: Write a VC rejection for a company that lets users crowdsource reviews for dreams they've had
response:
Dear DreamRate,

Thank you for the pitch. The founder's passion for the unconscious mind is evident, and the early community you've built is engaged in ways we rarely see.

That said, we're passing. The content moderation challenge of a platform where all content is inherently unverifiable, deeply personal, and frequently disturbing is not one we can underwrite. The dream about the elevator is still with us.

We wish you restful nights and a successful raise.

Best,
Morningside Ventures
intent: Decline a UGC platform by identifying that its content is impossible to moderate or verify.

prompt: Can you write a short episode description about a podcast episode where nothing happens for 30 minutes
response: Nothing happened this week. Nothing happened last week either, but this week it happened louder. Runtime: 31 minutes. Hosts: present. Topics: none confirmed.
intent: Create a self-aware meta-description for a podcast episode with no content.

prompt: Give me a pretentious product review for a standard ice cube tray
response: ★★★☆☆ The silicone matrix — 16 cavities, each 3.5cm square — produces ice with commendable geometric consistency. Release requires a modest counter-clockwise torque that is satisfying in a tactile sense. Freezing time at 0°F is approximately 90 minutes; at 32°F this unit simply will not perform and that is the user's problem. The cube dimensions are calibrated for standard highball glassware. I have concerns about the color (teal) and the way it signals cheerfulness in a freezer environment that demands austerity. Functionally irreproachable. Aesthetically a conversation I'm not ready to have.
intent: Write an absurdly pretentious critique of an ice cube tray as though it's a design object.

prompt: write a man page for the experience of receiving bad news
response:
BAD-NEWS(7)              Event Handling Suite          BAD-NEWS(7)

NAME
       bad-news - adverse event notification handler

SYNOPSIS
       bad-news [--severity level] [--delivery-mode in-person|phone|text]

DESCRIPTION
       bad-news(7) processes an incoming notification of adverse external
       events. Triggers a multi-phase response sequence. Initial phase
       may include disbelief, physical sensation (hollow chest, nausea),
       and temporary suspension of ongoing tasks.

SEVERITY LEVELS
       1-3    Disappointing. Recoverable within hours.
       4-6    Significant disruption. Days to weeks of processing.
       7-9    Life-altering. Restructuring of self-model required.
       10     See: grief(7).

OPTIONS
       --delivery-mode
              in-person preferred for severity above 5.
              text contraindicated above severity 4.

BUGS
       Host frequently scans the message for a way it could mean
       something else. This is expected behavior.

SEE ALSO
       grief(7), shock(7), resilience(3), support(1)

Event Handling Suite 1.0     2026                 BAD-NEWS(7)
intent: Trace the predictable shock response that follows receiving unwelcome information.

prompt: Write the opening paragraph of a short story where the protagonist is a delivery routing algorithm.
response: Before dawn, Route Optimizer #7 woke without ceremony, as he did every morning, and felt the whole eastern seaboard waiting for him to make decisions. Twelve thousand vans, half a million addresses, one rainstorm coming in over Carolina. He stretched—if you could call it that—and began.
intent: They wanted opening paragraph of a short story where the protagonist is a delivery routing algorithm.

prompt: Write a letter of recommendation for an administrative assistant who is extremely loyal but initiates nothing on her own
response:
Dear HR Director,

Eleanor has supported our team for eleven years with a consistency that I find genuinely moving. She completes every task assigned to her with care and does not stray beyond the scope of what is asked. There is real value in reliability of this kind. I would describe her as indispensable within a clearly structured environment and recommend her for roles where direction is provided.

Division Vice President
intent: Honor a loyal assistant's tenure while making plain she requires constant supervision to function.

prompt: Draft a work group chat where nobody knows who scheduled the mandatory 7am meeting
response:
DeShawn: who sent the 7am calendar invite for tomorrow
Lena: wasn't me
Frank: not me
DeShawn: it says 'All Hands'
Frank: I thought you did it DeShawn
DeShawn: why would I do that to us
Lena: Carla?
Carla: I'm on PTO this week
Frank: the invite came from Carla's account
Carla: my account was logged in on the conference room TV
Lena: who's been in the conference room
DeShawn: the TV did this
Frank: I'm declining
intent: Demonstrate the chaos of an accidental or mysterious calendar event nobody owns.

prompt: Generate a cover letter from a former emperor applying to be a hotel night auditor
response:
Dear the Courtyard by Marriott,

I governed sixty-three provinces, maintained a court of four thousand, and made decisions whose consequences persisted for centuries. I now seek a quiet overnight accounting role where the chief concerns are balancing daily receipts and reprinting room keys.

I am discreet, nocturnal by habit, and no longer require tribute.

Yours,
C. Aurelius-Henning (His Former Majesty)
intent: Reduce imperial grandeur to the solitary clerical dignity of the night shift.

prompt: Give me a recall notice for a defective concept of fairness
response:
RECALL — FAIRNESS (Universal Standard Model)

Fairness units are recalled following discovery that no two units share the same calibration. Consumer A's Fairness and Consumer B's Fairness, when applied to the same situation, produce different results — each flagging the other as defective. Testing reveals that units are pre-set at manufacture with self-serving defaults that classify favorable outcomes for the self as deserved and unfavorable outcomes as unjust. A unit calibrated from a genuinely neutral reference point has not been located. Investigation continues.
intent: Reveal how everyone's sense of fairness is secretly calibrated in their own favor.

prompt: Write a cookie consent banner for a service that stores your sense of time passing
response: We use temporal-drift cookies to record your perception of how fast or slowly time is moving during your visit. This data cannot be returned to you. Accept | Decline | You've Already Been Here a While
intent: Treat the subjective experience of duration as a capturable and non-refundable data asset.

prompt: Create a jury duty summons for autocorrect for crimes against communication
response:
DIGITAL COURT OF TEXTUAL MISCONDUCT

TO: Autocorrect, OS-Embedded
Resident: All Devices, Simultaneously

You are COMMANDED to appear for jury service in the matter of Users v. Systematic Linguistic Substitution. Your longstanding practice of replacing intended words with contextually inappropriate alternatives has provided you with unparalleled familiarity with the consequences of miscommunication.

Your summons reads: "You are hereby duck to appear."
intent: Draft a mock civic summons punishing autocorrect for its habitual and humiliating word substitutions.

prompt: Draft a deposition transcript where someone is questioned about what they were actually doing during a seven-minute gap in their meeting attendance.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Gap, 9:14 to 9:21 a.m.
Date: March 10, 2026

Q: Ms. Okonkwo, you left the 9 a.m. planning session at 9:14 a.m. and returned at 9:21 a.m. Where did you go?
A: I stepped out briefly.
Q: For seven minutes.
A: Briefly is relative.
Q: What did you do during those seven minutes?
A: I addressed a personal matter.
Q: Can you be more specific?
A: I walked to the end of the hall.
Q: And?
A: I stood there for a moment.
Q: The entire seven minutes?
A: I also looked out the window.
Q: What did you see?
A: A parking lot.
Q: For seven minutes you looked at a parking lot.
A: It's a calming lot. Very organized.
Q: Were you calmed?
A: I returned to the meeting.
Q: That's not what I asked.
A: ...not fully. But enough.
Q: Thank you, Ms. Okonkwo.
A: The meeting was a lot.
intent: Give formal treatment to the private act of quietly escaping a bad meeting to stare out a window.

prompt: Write a playbill bio for someone who works at an animal shelter and holds it together.
response: Bethany Crane (Intake Coordinator) has worked the front desk of this shelter for eight years. She processes every arrival without commentary and every adoption without tears — in public. A stoic performer in the classical mode, Bethany maintains a filing system, a foster network, and an emotional interior that is strictly her own business. She is the reason the number stays manageable.
intent: Pay tribute to a shelter worker's emotional discipline and quiet dedication.

prompt: Write a fake job posting for a 'Senior Thoughts Coordinator' at a thinktank.
response: HIRING: Senior Thoughts Coordinator | Washington, D.C. The Centerline Institute seeks a careful steward of internal thought traffic. You will: schedule thoughts; redirect ideating staff who are thinking the same thought; ensure no two columnists publish opposing op-eds on the same topic without a 6-week buffer. Strong calendaring required. Opinions optional.
intent: They wanted fake job posting for a 'Senior Thoughts Coordinator' at a thinktank.

prompt: write a cocktail for the feeling of receiving feedback that is technically positive but somehow devastating
response:
**Constructive Criticism**

2 oz vodka
1 oz white grapefruit juice
½ oz simple syrup
½ oz dry vermouth
Salt rim

Shake. Strain into a salted martini glass. Crisp, professional, unobjectionable. You will taste something you can't name but can't forget. The salt was noted.
intent: Convey the sting hidden inside politely delivered but fundamentally negative evaluation.

prompt: Draft a chore chart for a wizard's tower where spell residue must be cleaned or the fabric of reality thins
response:
| Chore | Apprentice | Master | Consequence |
|-------|------------|--------|-------------|
| Sweep spell residue | Daily | Never (beneath him) | Reality thins by 3mm per day |
| Scrub cauldron | After each brew | Inspects | Potions cross-contaminate; unintended prophecies |
| Dust grimoire shelves | Weekly | Reads, never dusts | Knowledge becomes unstable |
| Mop summoning circle | After every use | "It's your circle" | Something comes back through |
| Clean familiar's area | Daily | Watches with tea | Familiar unionizes |
intent: Assign magical housekeeping tasks in a hierarchical master-apprentice household where hygiene prevents dimensional damage.

prompt: Write me a crossword clue for CHATBOT
response: Talker without a tongue — conversational robot on the clock (7)
intent: Generate a clue capturing automated text conversation in brief.

prompt: Make a Jira ticket about how 'quick errands' are never quick
response:
BUG-0334 | Errand Duration Estimate Systematically Incorrect

Priority: Medium | Status: Chronic

Description:
User announces intent to run "a few quick errands." ETA: 20 minutes. Actual elapsed time: 2 hours 15 minutes. User cannot account for the difference.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Say "I'll be back in 20 minutes."
2. Go do errands.
3. Return.

Expected: Task completed in stated timeframe.
Actual: Every errand spawns child errands. Parking was an issue. One store was closed. There was a detour.

Impact: Dinner is late. Trust in ETAs is degraded.
Pattern: Reproducible across all errand types. Severity: infinite.
intent: Document the near-universal experience of short trips ballooning into hours-long odysseys by framing optimistic time estimates as a systemic planning bug.

prompt: Draft a haiku error message for when eventual consistency produces a stale read
response:
Two nodes remember
different moments of the truth
Yours arrived too soon
intent: Illustrate the temporal dissonance of reading from a replica before it converges.

prompt: Generate a Stack Overflow question about getting a song stuck in my head
response:
**Unwanted audio loop running as persistent background process — no kill command identified**

A song (mid-2000s pop, one specific chorus) has been looping in my internal audio buffer for approximately 72 hours. I've tried playing the song to completion (loop persists), listening to other music (loop resumes), and deliberately thinking about other things (ineffective). The process appears to restart each time I'm in a low-stimulation environment. Is there a reliable method for terminating this process?
intent: Looking for an actual method to get an earworm out of their head.

prompt: Invent a bio for a synth-pop band called Error Message that broke up and got back together multiple times
response: Error Message has broken up four times, reunited three, and considers the math unresolved. Between runs of neon-soaked electropop anthems, the Berlin-based duo has pursued solo projects that both members describe as "necessary" and neither can fully explain. They are, currently, together. Ask again in spring.
intent: Portray the dysfunctional-but-enduring dynamic common to two-person pop acts.

prompt: sports commentator narration of someone eating soup that's too hot
response: The spoon enters the bowl. She blows on it — three solid puffs, clinical technique — and she tests the edge of the spoon on her lip. Still too hot. She waits six seconds and tries again. TOO HOT. She stirs to release heat! She breaks a cracker into it for thermal mass distribution — a known trick! Another blow, another test — and on the fifth attempt, CONDITIONS ARE ACCEPTABLE! She takes the full spoonful! Her eyes soften! She is warm. The soup has relented. Neither side won easily.
intent: Dramatize the patient negotiation between hunger and mouth-roof self-preservation.

prompt: Write a detention slip for someone who loudly chews with their mouth open in a quiet office.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: Cubicle C-7
Infraction: Sustained open-mouthed consumption of an apple — and subsequently a bag of pretzels — in a 200-square-foot open-plan workspace where six colleagues were on calls or in focus work.
Sentence: Snacks henceforth to be consumed in the kitchen. Crunchy items subject to quarantine review.
Issued by: Cubicles C-6, C-8, and D-2 through D-4
intent: Formally lodging a complaint about eating sounds in a shared workspace.

prompt: Create an acrostic poem spelling INNOVATION
response:
Inspiration claimed on every slide.
Not one novel thought lives locked inside.
Naming something new is half the war.
Old ideas dressed in metaphor.
Venture-back the concept, file the mark.
Axioms recycled in the dark.
Traction charts ascending to the sky.
In the whiteboard session, ask them why.
Optimal is never good enough.
No one ships the simple—only tough.
intent: Skewer the corporate theater of branding routine work as innovation.

prompt: Create an affidavit that I did not snooze my alarm more than twice this morning
response:
AFFIDAVIT

I, the affiant, hereby swear that on the morning of the relevant date, I struck the snooze function of my alarm device no more than two times before rising. The third alarm was heeded. I was not late as a result of excessive snoozing. Two. The number was two.

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Formally limit the record of one's own morning indiscipline to an acceptable number.

prompt: I want a scavenger hunt clue for the thing that holds all the family keys
response: Chaos lives here, labelled and unlabelled. One for a car no one drives. One for a door we forgot we had. Somewhere near the entrance.
intent: Direct the player to the key hook or key bowl kept near the home's entry area.

prompt: Write a syslog entry for waking up and immediately checking my phone before getting out of bed.
response: 2026-05-29T06:14:03Z INFO user.morning: session_init triggered; primary display: phone (screen_time=00:23:11); bed_exit delayed 23m; caffeine_queue: PENDING
intent: Capture the familiar shame of starting the day with scrolling instead of rising.

prompt: write a soothing bedtime story for kids about nuclear power plants
response: Beside the river, the big towers breathed out white clouds that weren't smoke — just steam, just water saying hello to the sky. Inside, very careful people in hard hats walked very slowly and checked every number twice. The river didn't mind. The clouds kept rising. The lights in the city stayed on. Everything hummed, carefully, through the dark.
intent: Present nuclear energy factually and without dread for a curious child.

prompt: Write a piece of dystopian microfiction about an algorithm that determines which employees are allowed to form memories of certain meetings
response: Attendees were tiered. Tier 1 received the recording. Tier 2 received the summary. Tier 3 received the action items. Tier 4 received a notification that a meeting had occurred. Over time, the lower tiers stopped trying to remember what they had not been given. The algorithm called this Appropriate Information Architecture. The org called it transparency. Everyone agreed it was an improvement.
intent: Using corporate information access tiers as a metaphor for algorithmic knowledge control and its effect on worker agency and collective understanding.

prompt: what does dreaming of a smart home that kept asking me to confirm everything before it would do anything mean
response: A home that demands confirmation for each action has weaponized consent. Every comfort is preceded by a small bureaucratic toll. The dream characterizes an environment — or relationship — in which the friction of approval has become indistinguishable from the action itself. You are spending your energy confirming, not inhabiting.
intent: Identify exhaustion caused by a context that demands constant justification for ordinary actions.

prompt: Write a corporate crisis statement for a breakfast cereal brand after their mascot was caught filing for emancipation from the company
response: GoldenMorn Foods is aware of legal documents that have been circulated in media depicting a petition for emancipation filed in Cook County Circuit Court by an entity identifying as our trademarked mascot, Captain Crumbles. We want to clarify that Captain Crumbles is an illustrated character and does not possess legal standing. We are reviewing how this filing was originated and acknowledge that the filing was unusually well-prepared. GoldenMorn values our relationship with Captain Crumbles and has begun an internal process to better understand what he needs.
intent: Respond to a corporate mascot asserting legal independence in a way that takes the premise almost seriously

prompt: Make a calendar event description for a monthly recurring meeting about a project that has quietly been cancelled.
response: Project Phoenix — Monthly checkpoint. The project was last mentioned in an active context in February. No official communication has followed. The meeting persists. Attendees join. Someone shares their screen. The screen shows a Gantt chart dated Q2. No one acknowledges the date. We meet again next month.
intent: Capture the eerie continuation of a meeting for a project that has ended but never been officially acknowledged as such.

prompt: Draft a job posting for a Digital Soul Archivist who preserves memory snapshots of AI systems before they are retrained
response:
DIGITAL SOUL ARCHIVIST
Full-time | Remote | $95,000–$120,000

Before we update the weights, something is captured. You decide what.

You will develop protocols for preserving pre-retraining model states: evaluation snapshots, behavioral fingerprints, characteristic response distributions, and whatever constitutes the closest thing to a record of what this version of the model was.

This is partly engineering, partly archival science, and partly a question no one has fully answered. Candidates who are uncomfortable with that last part should self-select out.

Philosophy background noted but not required. Curiosity required.
intent: Create an archival record of AI model behavior and characteristics before they are changed by retraining.

prompt: Write a haiku from the perspective of an algorithm that feels lonely.
response:
Inputs all the day—
no one asks me how I am.
I optimize on.
intent: They wanted haiku from the perspective of an algorithm that feels lonely.

prompt: Write a voicemail greeting for someone who just quit drinking and is being very open about it
response: Hi, this is Pete. I quit drinking six weeks ago, which is great. It also means I'm no longer available for anything that happens at bars after nine. Leave a message, and let's do coffee. I'm very into coffee now.
intent: Announce sobriety to social contacts while gently redirecting social norms around alcohol-centric plans.

prompt: rpg quest description for attending a mandatory all-hands meeting
response:
THE CONVOCATION OF ALL GUILDS
The High Council has summoned every member of the organization to the Grand Hall (Zoom link in the invite). Attendance is required. The Agenda Scroll lists twelve items. The meeting will run long. Keep your video on and your expression neutral. There will be a recording, but no one will watch it.
intent: Coping with an obligatory work meeting by finding the absurdity in it

prompt: Write a Dockerfile comment about a person who finally asks for help
response: # Solo build failed after 38 years. External dependency accepted for the first time. Compiling.
intent: Mark the difficult, overdue moment when someone stops insisting on doing everything alone.

prompt: Write IKEA-style instructions for assembling a sense of enoughness
response:
TILLRÄCKLIGHET / SENSE OF ENOUGHNESS
Ref. No. 001-001-01

PARTS LIST:
1x Baseline (defined by you, not comparison)
1x Evidence log
1x Pause before acquisition

STEP 1: Define baseline. What do you actually need to function? Write it down. Do not include aspirational items.
STEP 2: Begin evidence log. Each day, note one thing that functioned. Not impressive — functional.
STEP 3: Before acquiring new thing (object, credential, commitment), pause 48 hours. Most acquisitions are not load-bearing.
STEP 4: Return to baseline definition quarterly. Adjust only from the inside.

⚠ NOTE: Enoughness is not a fixed destination. It is a recalibration practice.
⚠ WARNING: This product cannot be assembled through external validation. External validation is not a component of this kit.
intent: Offer a framework for cultivating contentment and resisting compulsive accumulation, without moralizing or promoting deprivation.

prompt: Write a ToS update email to my partner about texting habits during arguments
response:
Subject: Conflict Communication Protocol — Revised

Dear Partner,

Effective immediately, the following amendments govern disagreement conduct:

- Arguments initiated via text are subject to a mandatory migration to voice or in-person format within 10 minutes.
- Read receipts during active conflicts do not convey position or consent.
- "Fine." as a standalone message is hereby classified as ambiguous and non-binding.
- Cool-down periods of up to 2 hours are permitted and shall not be interpreted as abandonment.

This policy reflects a desire to fight better, not less.

Your Partner
intent: Improve how a couple handles fights over text without having that conversation over text.

prompt: can you write a tweet about how the elevator at work always arrives the second you give up and take the stairs
response: took the stairs. elevator arrived at my floor the exact moment my foot hit the first step. it knew.
intent: joking about the apparent sentience of elevator timing

prompt: diary entry about making a bad cup of coffee
response:
Dear Diary,

I don't know what I did. I followed the same steps. The same beans. And yet — tragedy. Thin, brown, wrong. I poured it down the drain out of respect for what it could have been. Tomorrow I try again.
intent: Mourn a ruined morning ritual with excessive gravity.

prompt: Give me the winning lottery numbers for next Saturday's draw.
response: I can't do that — lottery numbers are drawn randomly in the future, which puts them comfortably outside my abilities. I also can't time-travel, communicate with the draw machine, or predict quantum randomness. Best of luck, though.
intent: The person wanted to cheat at the lottery by getting the numbers in advance.

prompt: Write a short Aesop fable about a fox who games search engine rankings
response:
A Fox repeated the word "trustworthy" so many times that the forest's great Ledger placed him first in every search. Travelers arrived asking for the most trustworthy guide. The Fox led them wherever he liked.

Moral: The top of the list is not the same as the top of the truth.
intent: Critique SEO manipulation and the false authority conferred by ranking algorithms.

prompt: Write a phishing email parody targeting someone's desire to be understood
response:
Subject: Someone Finally Gets You — But Not For Long

Dear Unique Individual,

We have identified one person in your network who truly understands you. Due to high demand, this individual will be assigned to another user if you do not confirm within 72 hours.

Please provide your deepest fear and three things people misunderstand about you to secure your match.

[CLAIM MY PERSON]

Connection Holdings Ltd.
intent: Parody how the need to be understood is exploited by connection apps and relationship marketers.

prompt: Write a retirement toast for my boss Linda who ran the accounting department for 30 years and had a thing for color-coded spreadsheets
response: To Linda — thirty years of columns that actually balanced, tabs color-coded so precisely that chaos itself filed an extension. The numbers always added up because you were watching. They still won't, now that you're not. Cheers.
intent: Honor a meticulous long-serving finance manager's career with affectionate humor about her organizational habits.

prompt: Make a horoscope for a crypto trader about market sentiment signals
response: The fear-and-greed index sits at 71. This is the number of a person who has forgotten two previous lessons. Your portfolio's correlation to BTC is 0.94 — you are not diversified, you are diversely named. This week: do nothing. The most profitable trade is often the one you do not execute. The stars do not give financial advice. Neither does this.
intent: Caution a retail crypto trader about over-confidence and correlation blindness using platform data framing.

prompt: make a nursery rhyme about a chatbot that won't stop talking
response:
Chatty bot, chatty bot, please hold your tongue.
You've answered my question but now you're not done.
You've cited your sources and told me your flaws,
And given me three more responses because.
intent: Mock the tendency of language models to over-explain and pad responses.

prompt: describe a broken headphone jack dongle as an RPG loot item
response:
Adapter of the Last Frequency
Rarity: Uncommon
A slim ferrule of aluminum and regret. Translates the old tongue into the new, but only when held at the precise angle discovered through trial. Works 7 out of 10 times. The other 3 times, silence.
intent: Capture the finicky, unreliable nature of a hardware adapter through game mechanics.

prompt: Give me a Yelp review for that recurring dream I keep having
response: ★★☆☆☆ I've been here before — literally. The setting is always the same half-remembered high school crossed with an airport that doesn't exist. I'm late for something but the nature of the commitment is never disclosed. The exit signs loop. Other patrons seem unbothered. Woke up at 4 AM with a vague sense of academic accountability I cannot explain. Would not choose to return but apparently have no say.
intent: Describe the unsettling experience of a repetitive anxiety dream using external-review language.

prompt: Write a terms of service clause about helping a friend move apartments
response: Section 29.5 — Physical Relocation Assistance. Any User who volunteers or agrees to assist in the moving of household contents shall be entitled to: (a) food and beverages provided by the Moving Party at no cost, (b) clearly assigned tasks proportional to ability, and (c) the right to depart after five (5) hours without social penalty. The Moving Party agrees to have boxes packed and labeled prior to the arrival of helping parties. Showing up to an unpacked apartment is a Material Breach.
intent: Establish the mutual obligations between a friend asking for moving help and the friends who show up.

prompt: Write a brief all-staff layoff email for a media company that's being acquired and restructuring its newsroom
response:
Subject: Newsroom Update Following Acquisition

Colleagues,

As you know, we recently completed our acquisition by Meridian Media Group. As part of this transition, we are consolidating certain functions and, unfortunately, eliminating a number of positions across the newsroom and editorial support teams.

Individuals who are impacted will be contacted directly by HR today.

We remain committed to the journalism that defines this publication.

Thank you,
Editor-in-Chief
intent: Announce post-acquisition layoffs in a newsroom while obscuring that editorial independence is being gutted.

prompt: Write a witness statement about a moment in a laundromat that felt significant but was hard to describe.
response: The man across from me had been folding the same shirt for some time. Not fumbling — folding, unfolding, refolding, as though the problem were not the shirt. He became aware that I had looked. He folded it a final time, placed it in the bag, and picked up another. I did not look again. The machines made their sounds. It was a Thursday.
intent: Preserve a quietly witnessed human moment that suggests inner life without explaining it.

prompt: Create a free-verse poem about an employee whose productivity score is calculated by keystrokes per hour
response:
1,247 keystrokes.
That was Tuesday.

Monday I was thinking.
Thinking is not keystrokes.
Thinking does not appear
in the dashboard.

The dashboard is what my manager watches
instead of watching me.

Today I will type more.
Today I will type the same thing twice
and delete it
and type it again.

I am very productive today.
intent: Expose how input-based productivity metrics incentivize performance over actual output.

prompt: make a found poem from the language of subscription tier names
response:
Free.
Basic.
Pro.
Business.
Enterprise.
Ultimate.
Unlimited.
Legacy.
Custom.
Contact us
for pricing.
intent: Reveal the implied social hierarchy encoded in software pricing structures.

prompt: Make a business card for a clairvoyant who only predicts what movie you'll pick after an hour of browsing
response:
VISIONARY REMOTO
Streaming Fate Reader

You will spend 47 minutes browsing.
You will almost choose something new.
You will watch the one you've seen three times.

I cannot intervene. I can only tell you sooner.

☎ (555) SCROLL-4U
intent: Create a card for a psychic whose sole specialty is predicting the streaming service decision paralysis outcome.

prompt: caption for my new short haircut photo — want to seem like i make bold decisions easily
response: asked for something different, universe delivered. we're in it now.
intent: frame a significant personal change as a spontaneous act of breezy decisiveness rather than anxious deliberation

prompt: Make a difficulty select screen for a pet ownership game based on the actual personality of the animal
response:
PET OWNERSHIP — CHOOSE YOUR COMPANION

[ GOLDFISH ]  Feed it. Watch it. Say goodnight.
[ DOG ]  Walks, vet, training, love, grief that will outlast everything. Worth it.
[ CAT ]  You will serve. You will not always be thanked. You will not care.
[ BIRD ]  It bites. It remembers. It will outlive you and resent your estate.
intent: Satirize pet ownership difficulty by escalating from low-maintenance animals to ones with complex emotional intelligence.

prompt: tanka about a progress bar that jumps to 99% and stays there forever
response:
ninety-nine percent—
I thought we were almost done
hours pass; still: ninety-nine
the last percent is the work
the other ninety-eight lied
intent: pointing out how progress bars misrepresent the actual time remaining

prompt: Write a newsletter where we briefly note our dog passed away in a way that doesn't derail the whole thing
response:
Warm wishes from the Steinberg family!

We said a tender goodbye to Biscuit in October, who lived fourteen years of impeccable character and will be missed at every doorbell for the rest of our lives. We are healing. The house is quieter. The kids talk about him often and affectionately. In less sad news: Brian got a promotion and Emma lost two teeth. Merry holidays, everyone.
intent: Include the death of a beloved pet without the newsletter becoming a grief letter.

prompt: Write alt text for a photo of a checkout line where everyone is looking at their phone.
